title: What Humans Still Decide
concept: NAS still depends on human choices: people define the search space, choose the objective, set hardware or latency constraints, and decide what tradeoffs the system should optimize for.

options.text: Evaluating many candidate architectures can require lots of training and validation
NAS pays humans per convolution layer
NAS cannot be run by software at all

exercises.explain: Right — and because compute is precious, human choices about what to search become even more important.

cards.title: A search engine still needs a target
cards.body: If you tell someone 'go find the best route,' they will immediately ask: best by what measure? Shortest distance, fastest arrival, or cheapest tolls? NAS has the same issue. Before search starts, humans must decide what 'best' means.

cards.title: Human choices frame the whole search
cards.body: People choose the search space, the objective to optimize, and the constraints the model must obey. For a mobile vision app, humans might ask NAS to maximize accuracy while staying under a latency or memory budget. Those framing choices strongly shape the result.

cards.title: Like setting the rules of a tournament
cards.body: A tournament organizer decides the field, scoring rules, and time limits. The players then compete inside that setup. NAS works the same way: the algorithm explores options, but humans create the arena and choose how winners are judged.

cards.title: Different goals produce different 'best' architectures
cards.body: For a cloud classifier, a team might optimize mostly for accuracy. For a drone or phone, the team may care about latency, battery, and memory too. The same search method can produce different winners depending on the objective and constraints humans choose.

cards.title: NAS does not replace architectural judgment
cards.body: A searched architecture can be impressive, but the search inherits human assumptions. If the search space excludes a great block type, NAS cannot discover it. If the objective ignores latency, NAS may return a model that is accurate but impractical. Human framing still matters enormously.

cards.title: Who decides what in NAS?
cards.body: The useful mental model is partnership, not replacement.

left.text:
Define search space
Choose objective + constraints
Decide what deployment matters

right.text:
Explore candidates
Score and compare options
Surface strong architectures

exercises.prompt: Which decision is still mainly a human responsibility in NAS?

options.text: Choosing whether latency should matter along with accuracy
Making the validation images physically appear on the screen
Inventing a new color for every convolution layer
Guaranteeing the searched model is morally superior

exercises.explain: Right — the objective and deployment constraints are part of the human framing.

exercises.prompt: True or false: if a useful block type is left out of the search space, NAS can still discover it anyway.
exercises.explain: False — NAS can only search inside the space people provide.

exercises.sentence: Before NAS can search, humans must define the objective and the search ___.
exercises.explain: Exactly — the search space is part of the human setup.

exercises.prompt: Which scenario best shows a human decision shaping NAS results?

options.text: A phone-app team tells NAS to prefer models that stay under a strict latency budget
A student renames an image file after class
A printer changes from portrait to landscape mode

exercises.explain: Yes — the team's chosen constraint directly changes what NAS considers good.

exercises.prompt: Two teams use NAS on the same image task. Team A optimizes mostly for accuracy on a server. Team B optimizes for accuracy under strict phone latency and memory limits. Why might they end up with different architectures?

options.text: Because humans chose different objectives and constraints, so NAS searched for different definitions of 'best'
Because NAS ignores all goals and returns random networks
Because model architecture has nothing to do with deployment setting
Because constraints only matter after a product ships

exercises.explain: Exactly. NAS is guided by the human-framed objective, search space, and constraints. Next: with that in mind, when should you trust hand-designed model families, and when is search worth the trouble?
